conversation.content:
The search returned nothing useful. I'll continue from local evidence and well-established kinetics theory: the amyloid lag time scales as a negative power of monomer concentration (t_lag ∝ m0^(-γ)), and seeding shortens lag time. The breakthrough idea — making the gate inflection center shrink with monomer and seed — captures exactly this physics.

The key physical insight: the half-response/inflection time decreases with both m0 and M0. By making `center = c5/(1 + c9²·m0 + c6²·seed)`, higher m0 or seed crosses the cooperative threshold at a lower aggregate concentration, equivalent to an earlier onset. The denominator is ≥1 by construction (squared constants, non-negative features), so no singularity. I'll also drop the second variable exponent `m0^c4` from the secondary term to reduce ill-conditioning that prior fragile runs hit.

Here are my targeted changes:

    c = constant_symbols(9)

    monomer = x[1]
    seed = x[2]
